topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The attic air hung thick and dusty, smelling of aged paper and forgotten lavender. Liam dragged a trunk across the floor, the wood protesting with a groan. Inside, nestled beneath moth-eaten blankets, was a shocking collection: binders, loose sheets, notebooks, each one meticulously filled with homework from every grade he could remember. His mother, who had always been so focused on his academic achievements, had done this. Every single assignment, every test paper, preserved like relics. A tight knot formed in Liam’s chest, a hot, prickly feeling that made his jaw clench. He picked up a crumpled essay from middle school. His fingers tightened around the paper until the edges threatened to tear. "Of course," he muttered to the silent room, the sound sharp and brittle. "She wanted to keep everything. Fine. Let her have it." He shoved the trunk shut, the click echoing loudly in the quiet space, and started the long descent down the stairs, his shoulders rigid.

[story 2]

Maya stared at the overflowing filing cabinet in the spare room. It wasn't just papers; it was a chronological archive of her entire schooling, complete with red ink corrections and hastily scribbled answers. A slow, simmering resentment began to bubble up, a dull ache behind her ribs that sharpened with every glance at the organized chaos. She ran a hand through her hair, feeling the tension coiling in her neck. *Why?* The question was a low, venomous whisper in her mind. Her mother had curated this history, turning mundane schoolwork into some kind of trophy case. Maya walked over to a stack of geometry proofs, deliberately sliding one off the top. It landed with a soft thud. She didn't bother to put it back. "All that effort," she said, her voice dangerously level, "just to hoard it." She turned abruptly, the sound of her footsteps heavy and deliberate, leaving the room feeling colder than before.

[story 3]

When Sarah found the box tucked behind the seldom-used bookshelf, the sheer volume of completed work made her breath catch. It was an overwhelming display of her mother’s dedication—every worksheet, every completed project, cataloged and stored away. A strange, bitter satisfaction washed over Sarah, quickly followed by a surge of irritation. She felt a distinct tightening in her chest, a kind of acidic burn. She picked up a particularly tedious history report, the paper crisp beneath her fingertips. She didn't examine it; she just held it, feeling the weight of her mother’s meticulousness pressing down on her. "She thinks this is important," Sarah whispered to the empty hallway. The thought made her want to laugh, a short, ugly sound that caught in her throat before she forced herself to swallow it down. She carefully placed the box back, closing the lid with an audible snap, a gesture of final dismissal.
